mappings.rationaleAI: United States history from colonial times through Cold War institutions including NATO and the National Security Council broadly addresses the entire U.S. section (A), including industrialization, racism, and the NAACP, touching on every major period from post-World War I (A.1) through the end of the Cold War (A.5).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: Hoover's administration, the stock market crash, Calvin Coolidge's laissez-faire policies (A.1.c), Roosevelt's New Deal measures for economic recovery (A.2.a), and reform measures of the second New Deal including labor legislation (A.2.b).

Prohibition in the United States from the temperance movement through the Eighteenth Amendment era (1920-1933) and its repeal directly addresses prohibition, growth of organized crime, and the jazz age (A.1.b).

Ronald Reagan's presidency, supply-side economics, tax cuts, military buildup, and Cold War strategy directly address the Reagan administrations (A.4.f), including military spending and social welfare cuts (A.4.f.i) and anticommunist foreign policy (A.4.f.ii).

The American presidency as constitutional institution, the Executive Office, the National Security Council, and presidential transitions broadly inform the entire U.S. section (A), which is structured around successive presidential administrations.

Congressional structure, committee systems, and legislative processes connect to the power struggle between the Supreme Court and President over New Deal legislation (A.2.b.i) and the Watergate scandal's congressional dimensions (A.4.c.iii).

The Supreme Court's judicial review power and institutional evolution connect to judicial invalidation of New Deal legislation (A.2.b.i) and innovative decisions of the Warren court on civil rights (A.3.b.i).

Constitutional history including separation of powers, property rights, and individual rights connects to the New Deal constitutional controversies (A.2.b.i) and civil rights legislation under the Warren court (A.3.b.i).
Evolution of American political institutions, the two-party system, and civil rights broadly addresses the entire U.S. section (A), spanning successive administrations and policy debates.

U.S. foreign policy covering diplomacy, military conflicts, the Cold War, and NATO directly addresses isolationism and neutrality (A.2.c), containment policy and the Truman Doctrine (A.3.a), the late 20th century including Vietnam and detente (A.4), and the post-Cold War era (A.5).

Cold War origins, NATO formation, superpower rivalry, and Vietnam directly address containment of communism under Truman (A.3.a), the Eisenhower administrations (A.3.b), Kennedy and the Cuban missile crisis (A.4.a), and Cold War continuation through detente (A.4).

The mid-20th-century civil rights movement and grassroots participation directly address intensification of the civil rights movement and Civil Rights acts (A.3.b.i), the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (A.4.b.i), and protests and assassinations of Robert Kennedy and Martin Luther King Jr. (A.4.b.ii).

African American struggles for freedom, citizenship, and equality connect to the civil rights movement and Warren court decisions (A.3.b.i) and increasing alienation among minority groups with city and campus protests (A.4.b.ii).

Cultural production and national identity formation including literature, music, film, and television connect to social conditions in the 1920s and the jazz age (A.1.b), though the coverage extends far beyond this period.

Women's suffrage, evolving legal rights, and political participation connect to increasing alienation among minority groups and protests in cities and on campuses (A.4.b.ii), reflecting broader social movements of the 1960s.
History of immigration to the United States including legal efforts to restrict immigration directly addresses restriction of immigration under Harding and Coolidge (A.1.a).

Asian American history including exclusion laws, discrimination, and World War II Japanese American incarceration connects to immigration restriction policies (A.1.a) and wartime mobilization (A.2.d).
The two-party system, electoral processes, and voter participation broadly inform the entire U.S. section (A), which is structured around successive presidential administrations and party politics.

World War II covering the global conflict, the Pacific theater including Japan, and Allied victory directly addresses U.S. wartime mobilization, the role of U.S. forces in defeating Axis powers, and military occupation of Japan and Germany (A.2.d).

Atomic and hydrogen bombs, Cold War arms race, and Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative address the large postwar military establishment (A.3.a.v), the Cuban missile crisis and Nuclear Test-Ban Treaty (A.4.a), and SALT talks (A.4.d).

Four hundred years of American military development, emergence as a global power after World War II, and challenges of professional forces address U.S. forces in World War II (A.2.d), the Korean War (A.3.a.iv), and the large postwar military establishment (A.3.a.v).
Terrorism including the September 2001 attacks and ISIS directly addresses the September 11 attacks under the George W. Bush administration (A.5.c).
The 1991 Gulf War and 2003 invasion directly address the Persian Gulf War under George H.W. Bush (A.5.a) and the Second Persian Gulf War under George W. Bush (A.5.c).

Canadian history covering political development, cultural diversity, Quebec separatism, and peacekeeping directly addresses Canada between the World Wars (B.1), wartime participation (B.2), and postwar developments including Franco-Canadian relations (B.3).

Capitalism including the Great Depression of the 1930s and financial systems connects to favouritism toward big business under Harding and Coolidge (A.1.a) and the stock market crash and Great Depression (A.1.c).
Neoliberal deregulation, privatization, and the 2008 financial crisis connect to Reagan's economic policies and social welfare cuts (A.4.f.i) and the post-Cold War era (A.5).

Diplomatic history including Versailles, wartime summitry, and the Iraq War addresses foreign policy between the World Wars (A.2.c), Cold War containment (A.3.a), and the lead-up to the Iraq War (A.5.c).

Globalization including trade, migration, and the Trump presidency connects to the Clinton administration's NAFTA ratification (A.5.b) and U.S.-Canadian economic relations and free-trade agreement (B.3.a).
Progressivism as reform movement, Theodore Roosevelt, and Woodrow Wilson provide historical context for the post-World War I Republican administrations (A.1.a) that reversed Progressive-era policies.
American business enterprise and corporate evolution provide context for favouritism toward big business and Coolidge prosperity (A.1.a).
Communism and Cold War confrontation with the United States address the Truman Doctrine's containment of communism (A.3.a.i) and McCarthyism and the Red Scare (A.3.a.iii).
U.S. involvement in the Middle East, including the Camp David Accords, is examined in A.4.e.
Federalism with primary focus on the United States, covering power-sharing between national and subnational governments, provides institutional context for the entire U.S. political section (A).
Indigenous peoples of North America and contemporary challenges facing Native American communities connect to Canada's relations with Indians, Eskimo, and Metis (B.3.d).
